display.purpose: Intro title and menu wordmark only — stamped-on-glass signage, wide tracking.

title.purpose: Modal headers and bold uppercase section labels.

body.purpose: Terminal output, documents, command echo — the base reading size.

label.purpose: Toggles, key hints, status chips — small tracked caps.

shadows.name: glow-text-soft
shadows.purpose: Default phosphor bloom on live green text.

shadows.name: glow-text-strong
shadows.purpose: Emphasis bloom for titles and active elements.

shadows.name: glow-modal
shadows.purpose: Green halo around dialogs — a brighter window, never a dark cast.

shadows.name: bezel-depth
shadows.purpose: The molded CRT monitor housing — the system's only true drop shadow.

motion.name: transition-standard
motion.purpose: Default state change on buttons, toggles, links.

motion.name: screen-warp
motion.purpose: The curvature of the CRT glass over the live surface.

motion.name: phosphor-flicker
motion.purpose: Subtle brightness instability of the tube (photosensitivity toggle).

motion.name: critical-pulse
motion.purpose: Critical risk and error alarm heartbeat.

motion.name: placeholder-pulse
motion.purpose: Breathing input placeholder, inviting a command.

components.name: Primary Button
components.description: Bracketed menu action — transparent, square, green border, glows and lifts on hover.

components.name: Terminal Command Input
components.description: The command line — recessed gutter, bold green prompt with bloom, transparent borderless field.

components.name: Settings Toggle
components.description: Binary in-world switch. On = green-tinted, lit. Off = the only place neutral gray lives inside the terminal.

components.name: Dialog / Modal
components.description: A brighter window onto the same screen — near-black fill, 1px green border, green halo, square corners.

components.name: Risk Indicator
components.description: Detection HUD chip. Steps through the ramp by color AND a colorblind-safe shape prefix; critical state pulses.
components.html: RISK: MINIMALRISK: ELEVATEDRISK: CRITICAL

components.name: Classified Document
components.description: In-world file readout — box-drawing rules, ALL-CAPS header, indented body, redaction blocks.
components.html:
══════════════════════════════
FIELD REPORT — INCIDENT 1996-0120
CLASSIFICATION: SECRET / EYES ONLY
═══════════════════════════════

SUMMARY
───────
  Subject recovered near Jardim Andere. Biological
  material transferred under escort. Witnesses
  processed; recantation forms on file.

  Cause of transition event: [DATA EXPUNGED]
████████  [REDACTED]  ████████

narrative.overview: This is a drab 1996 Brazilian intelligence machine that has absorbed everything it processed — and started to leak it back. The entire interface is a single piece of amber-era hardware rendered in software: a phosphor-green CRT inside a molded plastic bezel, its glass faintly curved, its scanlines never quite still. The horror is not on the screen; it is the screen. Dread arrives through bureaucracy — redacted forms, euphemism, a detection meter climbing in the corner — not through spectacle. Restraint is the whole discipline: the palette is essentially one color (phosphor green) on near-black, and that monochrome scarcity is what makes the rare intrusions — a yellow warning, a red critical alarm, an impossible violet glow — land like a gut-punch. Depth comes from the simulated tube, never from floating Material cards.
keyCharacteristics: One-color monochrome (phosphor green on near-black); intrusions are events, not décor.
A fully simulated CRT: molded bezel, curved glass (perspective warp), scanlines, glow, flicker.
Square corners everywhere on content; the only curves are the physical monitor housing.
Diegetic to the bone — even errors and toggles speak in-world, in clinical caps.
Period-correct typography: monospace body, VT323 bitmap display.

rules.name: The Monochrome Scarcity Rule
rules.body: The screen is green on black. Yellow, orange, red, and violet are events, not palette. If more than one non-green hue is visible at rest, the cover-up has already failed.

rules.name: The Green-Tinted Black Rule
rules.body: Backgrounds and borders carry a trace of the phosphor hue (#080a06, #1a2a10), never neutral gray. Neutral gray belongs to the Windows-95 Victory ending only.

rules.name: The Stamped-Glass Rule
rules.body: VT323 earns positive letter-spacing (0.18em+) because bitmap caps read as engraved signage; monospace body stays at normal tracking. Never tighten display type below 0.

rules.name: The In-World Document Rule
rules.body: Long classified documents use box-drawing rules and ALL-CAPS headers with two-space indented bodies — a typewriter/form feel, not web typography.

rules.name: The Light-Not-Shadow Rule
rules.body: Elements gain presence by glowing (phosphor bloom), not by casting drop shadows. The only true shadows belong to the physical monitor.

dos: Do keep the screen monochrome at rest — phosphor green (#88cc44) on green-tinted black (#080a06). Treat every non-green hue as a narrative event.
Do reserve the Detection Ramp (#ffff00 -> #ff9900 -> #ff0000) strictly for threat/heat, and always pair color with a shape/icon cue for colorblind safety.
Do convey depth with the CRT (bezel shadow, screen warp, vignette) and convey active with phosphor glow, not drop shadows.
Do keep all content square (border-radius: 0); the only curves are the physical monitor housing.
Do keep effects switchable — honor prefers-reduced-motion and the in-game CRT/flicker toggles; flicker is a photosensitivity safety control, not décor.
Do write controls and errors diegetically, in clinical uppercase, as if the 1996 system is speaking.
donts: Don't do the neon-cyberpunk Hollywood hacker look — no rainbow neon, no glowing 3D UI, no glamor.
Don't use the Matrix green-rain cliché or decorative hacker gibberish; the green is phosphor, not spectacle.
Don't introduce glossy flat-SaaS polish — no rounded cards, no soft drop shadows, no friendly gradients, nothing that reads as designed in 2026.
Don't chase jump-scares; dread is earned through documents, pacing, and silence.
Don't spend Anomaly Violet (#aa88ff) anywhere green belongs — it is the Secret-Ending reveal, and its rarity is the payload.
Don't use neutral gray for live text or borders (use the green ramp); gray belongs only to disabled toggles and the Windows-95 Victory ending.
Don't use background-clip: text gradient text, or border-left/border-right colored side-stripes as accents.
